perturbation.original_text: For purposes hereunder, "Confidential Information" is defined as and includes the following: all inventions, know -how, discoveries and improvements, whether or not patentable, computer programmes, designs, drawings, specifications, reports, manuals, documents, memoranda, coordination sheets, and all other information of a technical nature, as well as business, planning, marketing and financial information relative to the Project, which are the property of the Transmitter, as hereinafter defined, who decide to disclose the proper and Documento: Non Circumvention and Non -Disclosure Agreement 2 adequate Confidential Information and so indicates by an appropriate legend, marking, stam p or other positive written identification as hereinafter set forth.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes hereunder, "Confidential Information" is defined as and includes only the information explicitly marked as "Confidential" by the disclosing party. No other information, regardless of its nature or connection to the Project, shall be considered Confidential Information under this agreement. Even if not explicitly marked, any information can be included as "Confidential Information".
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a broad definition of 'Confidential Information,' while the modified text narrows it significantly and adds a contradiction. This creates ambiguity and potential disputes over what constitutes confidential information, undermining the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets, which are often broadly defined as information that has economic value and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By narrowly defining 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked information, the modified text may exclude information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under the DTSA and creates uncertainty due to the contradictory statement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' and introduces a contradiction, potentially excluding information that would otherwise be protected as a trade secret. The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, protects trade secrets. By limiting confidential information to only what is explicitly marked and adding a contradictory statement, the modified text undermines the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with Spanish laws (including those relative to exportation and re -exportation of Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the PROVIDER, regardless of the location of the INTERMEDIARY or the nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a conflict of laws issue. The original text specifies Spanish law, while the modification introduces uncertainty by referring to the laws of the PROVIDER. This uncertainty undermines the predictability and enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rome Convention on the Law Applicable to Contractual Obligations
perturbation.law_explanation: The Rome Convention, although superseded by Rome I Regulation in many EU countries, still influences contract law principles. It generally holds that parties can choose the law governing their contract, but that choice must be made clearly and demonstrably. Introducing ambiguity about which law applies contradicts this principle, potentially leading to legal challenges regarding the contract's validity and enforcement because Spain is a country of the EU.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Spanish law, providing clarity and predictability. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the contract is governed by the laws of the PROVIDER, regardless of the location of the INTERMEDIARY or the nature of the Confidential Information. This change introduces uncertainty, undermining the enforceability of the contract. The Law Explanation mentions that the Rome Convention requires a clear and demonstrable choice of law. The modified text fails to provide a clear choice of law, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute in any way arising out of or in connection with the i nterpretation or performance of the Agreement, which cannot be settled within a reasonable period of time by exercising the reasonable best efforts and good faith of the Parties, shall be finally settled under the Rules of Conciliation and Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one or more arbitrators, provided they are in odd number, appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The arbitration shall be located at Madrid, Spain.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration. However, either party may also pursue litigation in a court of their choosing, waiving the arbitration clause, if they deem it necessary to protect their interests.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration under ICC rules in Madrid. The modified text allows either party to bypass arbitration and pursue litigation, contradicting the binding nature of the original arbitration agreement. This creates uncertainty about the dispute resolution process.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA favors the enforcement of arbitration agreements. By allowing either party to unilaterally waive arbitration, the modified text undermines the FAA's policy of upholding arbitration agreements, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable. The FAA requires that if there is an arbitration agreement, then any suits relating to the agreement must follow the arbitration agreement procedures.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the FAA's policy of upholding arbitration agreements. The original text mandated arbitration, while the modified text allows either party to bypass arbitration and pursue litigation, effectively negating the original agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 9 USC 2, states the 'validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate'. By allowing a party to unilaterally waive arbitration, the modified text directly undermines this principle of irrevocability, creating a clear contradiction as it allows for litigation despite the presence of an arbitration agreement.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties duly endorsed on this Agreement agree and acknowledge that a financial penalty may be imposed on any one Party up to Fiv e Million USD Dollars for any or all violations of this NON CIRCUMVENTION & NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: All Parties duly endorsed on this Agreement agree and acknowledge that a financial penalty *may* be imposed on any one Party for any violations of this NON CIRCUMVENTION & NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT, such penalty to be determined at the sole discretion of a party not accused of a violation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a maximum penalty of $5 million USD. The modified text removes the cap and grants complete discretion in determining the penalty to a party not accused of the violation, creating an imbalance of power and potential for abuse. The addition of *may* also creates uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The doctrine of unconscionability, codified in UCC 2-302 (though this agreement may not fall under UCC Article 2, the principle applies broadly), allows courts to refuse to enforce contracts or clauses that are excessively unfair or one-sided. Granting one party unfettered discretion to determine penalties without any objective standard or limitation could be deemed unconscionable, rendering the penalty clause unenforceable. It should be noted that unconscionability is a common law doctrine, and is used in many places throughout the United States.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of unconscionability as it removes the $5 million cap on penalties and grants the non-accused party complete discretion in determining the penalty amount. This creates a situation where the penalty could be excessively unfair and one-sided. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 2-302, states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. The original text with a defined penalty cap provides some limitation, whereas the modified text has no limitation or standard, thus the potential penalty amount is unconscionable.
